title: for Loop
description: The for loop's three-part header — initialization, condition, increment — a full dry run, and the classic off-by-one and infinite-loop mistakes.
objectives: Write a basic for loop with correct syntax
Understand the three parts of a for loop's header and their execution order
Dry run a for loop step by step
Avoid off-by-one errors and accidental infinite loops

sections.text: This is the concept from the Loops overview, written in exact C++ syntax — all three pieces (start, condition, move forward) live in one header, separated by semicolons.

rows: Creates a counter i, starting at 1
Continue only while i is 5 or less

points: Check i <= 5 → 1 <= 5, true → run body → prints 1
Check i <= 5 → 2 <= 5, true → run body → prints 2
i++ → i becomes 3 ... this repeats through i = 5
After printing 5, i++ makes i become 6

sections.text: The condition is always checked before the body runs, including the very last time — that's why the loop stops after printing 5 rather than running one extra time.

Swapping <= for < changes how many times the loop runs — i < 5 stops as soon as i reaches 5, so 5 itself never prints. This one-character difference is behind one of the most common bugs in programming: the off-by-one error. Always double-check whether a boundary value should be included.

i starts at 1 and i <= 5 is already true, but i-- moves i further away from 5, not closer — i becomes 0, then -1, then -2, forever. i <= 5 stays true endlessly, so this never stops. The increment step must actually move the counter toward making the condition false eventually.
A for loop isn't limited to counting upward — starting high, checking >= instead of <=, and decrementing with i-- counts down just as naturally.

This is only a preview — a for loop can contain another for loop inside it, running the inner loop completely for every single repetition of the outer one. We'll explore nested loops properly in a dedicated topic, since they're central to many DSA patterns (like working through a grid).

sections.title: Checking every seat in a row
sections.text: "For seat 1 through seat 20, check if it's occupied" is a for loop in plain English: a clear starting seat, a clear stopping point, and a clear step (move to the next seat). Anytime a task has a known, countable range like this, a for loop is usually the natural fit.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using < when <= was intended (or vice versa) — causes an off-by-one error, running one time too few or too many.
Incrementing in the wrong direction for the condition — e.g. i-- with i <= 5, which never makes the condition false, causing an infinite loop.
Forgetting that the condition is checked before every repetition, including what would be the last one.
Re-declaring the same counter name in a nested loop's outer and inner headers without meaning to shadow it — using i for both outer and inner loops is a common source of confusion.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What are the three parts of a for loop's header?
questions.answer: Initialization (runs once before the loop starts), condition (checked before every repetition), and increment (runs after every repetition).

questions.question: In for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++), why does the loop stop after printing 5?
questions.answer: After the body runs with i = 5, i++ makes i become 6, and the condition i <= 5 is then checked and found false, stopping the loop before another repetition.

questions.question: What causes an infinite loop in a for statement?
questions.answer: When the increment step never actually moves the counter toward making the condition false — for example, decrementing a counter while the condition only becomes false for larger values.

questions.question: What is an off-by-one error?
questions.answer: A common bug where a loop runs one time too many or too few, often caused by mixing up < and <=, or miscounting the starting or ending boundary.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A for loop's header has three parts: initialization, which runs once; a condition, checked before every repetition; and an increment, which runs after every repetition. For for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++), the loop prints 1 through 5 and stops once i becomes 6 and the condition fails. Two classic bugs come from this structure: off-by-one errors, from mixing up < and <=, and infinite loops, when the increment moves the counter in a direction that never makes the condition false. for loops are the natural choice whenever the number of repetitions is known ahead of time.

sections.title: Trick to Remember for Loops
sections.text: for (start; check; step) — Start once, Check before every run, Step after every run. Wrong direction on step = infinite loop. Off by one symbol (< vs <=) = off-by-one bug.
